schema.detection_signal: primary signals an on-call sees first; one or more of: latency, errors, saturation, silence, cost, data_anomaly, security_alert
schema.onset_shape: one of: cliff (sudden), gradual (creeping), periodic (recurring pattern), flapping (intermittent)
schema.correlation: what the incident timeline correlates with; one of: deploy, config_change, load, time_of_day, scheduled_job, external_event, date_boundary, random
schema.blast_radius: one of: single_host, single_shard, single_service, dependency_subtree, subset_of_users, everything
schema.tags: free-form facets, sorted most-important-first; 'external' first marks causes outside the team's control

root_causes.name: Memory leak
root_causes.description: Process RSS grows until OOM-killed; often gradual, restarts temporarily fix it.

root_causes.name: Disk full
root_causes.description: Logs, temp files, or data fill the volume; writes fail, databases go read-only.

root_causes.name: Connection pool exhaustion
root_causes.description: All database or HTTP pool slots are held by slow queries or leaked connections; new requests queue or fail.

root_causes.name: Thread pool exhaustion
root_causes.description: Threads blocked on a slow downstream or sync-over-async calls starve request handling.

root_causes.name: File descriptor exhaustion
root_causes.description: A socket or file handle leak hits the process ulimit; new connections and file opens fail.

root_causes.name: Port exhaustion
root_causes.description: High connection churn or a full connection-tracking table exhausts network-layer connection slots; new connections fail or are silently dropped.

root_causes.name: CPU saturation
root_causes.description: A hot loop, pathological regex, or load growth pins all cores; latency balloons everywhere.

root_causes.name: GC pauses
root_causes.description: The runtime stops the world to collect garbage; latency spikes intermittently while CPU and error rates look almost normal.

root_causes.name: Missing index
root_causes.description: A query plan does full scans that were fine in staging but die at production data volume.

root_causes.name: N+1 queries
root_causes.description: Code issues one query per row instead of one per request; fine in dev, floods the database at production data volume.

root_causes.name: Query plan regression
root_causes.description: A statistics update or data skew flips the optimizer to a catastrophically worse plan with no code change.

root_causes.name: Lock contention
root_causes.description: Long transactions or hot rows serialize writes; lock waits and deadlocks cascade into timeouts.

root_causes.name: Hot partition
root_causes.description: A single counter row or shard key takes all the traffic while the rest of the cluster idles.

root_causes.name: Replication lag
root_causes.description: Read replicas fall behind the primary; users see stale data or read-your-write failures.

root_causes.name: Failed failover
root_causes.description: The primary dies and promotion fails, or two nodes both accept writes and diverge.

root_causes.name: Database maintenance lag
root_causes.description: Background housekeeping (vacuum, compaction) falls behind; scans slow down, disk usage grows, and in the worst case the database stops writes to protect itself.

root_causes.name: Blocking schema migration
root_causes.description: A schema change takes an exclusive lock on a hot table; every query queues behind it.

root_causes.name: Storage corruption
root_causes.description: A bad disk or firmware bug corrupts pages; specific reads error while everything else works.

root_causes.name: Bad code deploy
root_causes.description: A logic bug ships to production; the classic. Errors correlate tightly with the deploy timestamp.

root_causes.name: Bad config change
root_causes.description: A typo'd config or wrong env value ships; configs often skip the CI rigor that code gets.

root_causes.name: Feature flag misfire
root_causes.description: A flag is flipped to the wrong cohort or 100%, or the flag service goes down and everyone reverts to defaults.

root_causes.name: Bad data migration
root_causes.description: A migration corrupts or drops data, or runs an unbounded UPDATE that hammers the database.

root_causes.name: Version skew
root_causes.description: A partial rollout leaves incompatible old and new versions talking to each other.

root_causes.name: Serialization incompatibility
root_causes.description: A new version changes the wire or cache serialization format; old readers choke on new payloads.

root_causes.name: Dependency bump breakage
root_causes.description: A dependency update, often transitive, changes behavior subtly and breaks production.

root_causes.name: Rollback failure
root_causes.description: The rollback path is blocked by an irreversible migration or a broken prior artifact, extending the outage.

root_causes.name: Secret rotation failure
root_causes.description: A new credential isn't propagated everywhere; auth failures spread as caches and tokens expire.

root_causes.name: DNS failure
root_causes.description: An expired domain, bad record push, or resolver outage makes services unreachable by name.

root_causes.name: TLS certificate expiry
root_causes.description: A certificate isn't renewed and clients hard-fail; internal service-mesh certs count too.

root_causes.name: Load balancer misconfig
root_causes.description: A bad health check or wrong target group causes the LB to drop healthy backends or route unevenly.

root_causes.name: Internet infrastructure outage
root_causes.description: Routing, CDN edge, or ISP infrastructure between you and your users fails; your systems are healthy but partly or wholly unreachable.

root_causes.name: Firewall rule change
root_causes.description: A rule change blocks legitimate service-to-service traffic, often discovered path by path.

root_causes.name: Network partition
root_causes.description: A link failure splits the cluster; quorum systems degrade or risk split-brain.

root_causes.name: Retry storm
root_causes.description: Retries without backoff and jitter multiply load on a struggling dependency, preventing recovery.

root_causes.name: Thundering herd
root_causes.description: Mass simultaneous reconnects or refreshes after a restart or expiry overwhelm the system.

root_causes.name: Cache stampede
root_causes.description: A hot cache key expires and thousands of requests recompute it against the database simultaneously.

root_causes.name: Circuit breaker misbehavior
root_causes.description: A breaker stays open and rejects healthy traffic, or never opens and provides no protection.

root_causes.name: Queue backlog
root_causes.description: Producers outpace consumers; processing delay grows unbounded and downstream freshness degrades.

root_causes.name: Poison pill message
root_causes.description: One malformed message crashes consumers in a loop, blocking its partition indefinitely.

root_causes.name: Duplicate processing
root_causes.description: A non-idempotent consumer plus at-least-once delivery produces double charges, duplicate emails, or repeated side effects.

root_causes.name: Leader election failure
root_causes.description: The coordination service is unhealthy; the cluster has no leader or leadership flaps continuously.

root_causes.name: Clock skew
root_causes.description: NTP drift breaks TLS validation, token expiry checks, and distributed ordering assumptions.

root_causes.name: Stale cache
root_causes.description: Users see old data because writes don't propagate to cached reads; one of the two hard problems.

root_causes.name: Cache hit-rate collapse
root_causes.description: A restart, flush, or eviction pressure from an undersized cache empties it; the full read load lands on the database, which can't take it.

root_causes.name: Cloud provider outage
root_causes.description: An availability zone, region, or core managed service goes down; blast radius depends on your redundancy.

root_causes.name: Vendor outage
root_causes.description: A payment, auth, messaging, or artifact provider fails or throttles you; features break or deploys are blocked through no fault of your code.

root_causes.name: Upstream contract change
root_causes.description: A vendor changes response schema or behavior without notice; your parsing or logic silently breaks.

root_causes.name: DDoS
root_causes.description: A volumetric or application-layer flood exhausts bandwidth, connections, or compute.

root_causes.name: Credential compromise
root_causes.description: A leaked key, often from a public repo, is used to access, abuse, or destroy resources.

root_causes.name: Vulnerability exploitation
root_causes.description: An RCE, SQLi, or SSRF class vulnerability is actively exploited against your systems.

root_causes.name: Supply chain attack
root_causes.description: A compromised upstream package ships malicious code into your build and out to production.

root_causes.name: Ransomware
root_causes.description: An attacker encrypts data or deletes infrastructure; recovery depends entirely on backups and isolation.

root_causes.name: Cryptomining compromise
root_causes.description: An attacker's miners consume your compute; presents as a CPU and cloud-bill anomaly before anything else.

root_causes.name: Accidental data exposure
root_causes.description: A public bucket, open dashboard, or PII in error responses exposes sensitive data; a compliance incident.

root_causes.name: Write-path corruption
aliases: Bug writing wrong values at scale
root_causes.description: A bug writes incorrect values at scale; damage compounds until someone notices the anomaly.

root_causes.name: Accidental deletion
root_causes.description: A destructive command runs against production instead of staging; data or infrastructure vanishes instantly.

root_causes.name: Backup restore failure
root_causes.description: Backups were silently broken or never tested; discovered mid-incident when the restore is needed.

root_causes.name: Sensitive data in logs
root_causes.description: Sensitive data flows into the logging pipeline and everywhere logs are shipped; a compliance incident.

root_causes.name: Schema drift in pipeline
root_causes.description: An upstream field change silently breaks or poisons downstream ETL; dashboards quietly go wrong.

root_causes.name: Host failure
root_causes.description: A single host dies cleanly (disk, NIC, RAM) or degrades subtly (kernel bug, bad driver, noisy neighbor); painful when the workload wasn't redundant.

root_causes.name: Container node issues
root_causes.description: A bad node pool, disk-pressure evictions, or a sick node agent cause containers to churn and reschedule.

root_causes.name: Autoscaler misbehavior
root_causes.description: The autoscaler scales down too aggressively, hits its max, or flaps; capacity mismatches demand.

root_causes.name: Cloud quota hit
root_causes.description: You can't launch instances or make control-plane calls precisely when you most need capacity.

root_causes.name: Permissions change
root_causes.description: A role or policy edit breaks service-to-service auth, often in ways that surface minutes to hours later.

root_causes.name: Resource limits misconfigured
aliases: OOMKill loop from low memory limit
root_causes.description: A container memory limit set too low causes an OOMKill loop; a tight CPU limit causes invisible throttling.

root_causes.name: Race condition
root_causes.description: A concurrency bug manifests only under load or rare timing; unreproducible in dev by definition.

root_causes.name: Application deadlock
root_causes.description: A lock-ordering bug freezes threads holding each other's locks; the process is alive but does nothing.

root_causes.name: Infinite loop
root_causes.description: A specific input triggers a loop that spins CPU forever or recursion that blows the stack.

root_causes.name: Edge-case input crash
root_causes.description: A specific input — null, empty, unicode, emoji — hits an unhandled path and crashes or corrupts output for a slice of traffic.

root_causes.name: Integer overflow
root_causes.description: A 32-bit sequence or counter hits its max; inserts fail or values wrap to negative.

root_causes.name: Time bug
root_causes.description: A DST transition, leap day or second, epoch rollover, or timezone math error fires at a specific instant.

root_causes.name: Organic traffic spike
root_causes.description: A viral moment, marketing email, or TV mention brings legitimate but overwhelming traffic.

root_causes.name: Bot traffic
root_causes.description: Aggressive crawlers hammer expensive endpoints, consuming capacity out of proportion to their number.

root_causes.name: Rate limiter misconfig
root_causes.description: Rate limiting is absent (no protection) or too tight (blocking legitimate users).

root_causes.name: Load test against prod
aliases: Load test pointed at wrong environment
root_causes.description: An internal load test is pointed at the wrong environment and stress-tests production for real.

root_causes.name: Monitoring outage
root_causes.description: The monitoring stack itself fails: you're flying blind, or an outage happens and nobody is paged.

root_causes.name: Alert storm
aliases: Bad alert rule firing hundreds of times
root_causes.description: A bad alert rule pages hundreds of times or routes pages to nobody; the real signal drowns.

root_causes.name: Log volume explosion
root_causes.description: Debug logging left enabled or an error loop floods the pipeline, disk, and the observability bill.

root_causes.name: Runaway cron job
root_causes.description: A scheduled job overlaps its previous run, processes the wrong data, or saturates the database at the same time daily.

root_causes.name: Bad remediation
root_causes.description: Auto-healing or an on-call's fix script amplifies the incident, e.g. a mass restart creating a thundering herd.

root_causes.name: Expired credentials
root_causes.description: An API key, token, or license with an expiration date nobody tracks lapses and hard-fails at a specific moment.
